identified_error: The paper’s key methodological error was its use of a population-level cosinor seasonal adjustment to ‘predict’ each individual patient’s vitamin D status at hospitalization (and annual mean) from a single historical 25(OH)D measurement, while measurements could be taken anywhere from 14 to 730 days before infection. The model assumes every person shares the same seasonal sinusoidal pattern/amplitude and that the only change over time is this common seasonal shift. This is not valid for individual-level reconstruction of vitamin D exposure over intervals of up to 2 years. The paper then treated these derived values as if they were accurate patient-specific exposures and used them to support causal claims.
why_fatal: This error is fatal because the study’s central claim is about pre-infection vitamin D status as an independent predictor of COVID-19 severity. But for many patients, the exposure was not actually known at or near infection: it was extrapolated from old values using an invalid individual-level prediction method. A single vitamin D value measured months or even years earlier cannot reliably represent status at infection, and a group-average seasonal curve cannot recover each person’s true later level. That creates substantial exposure misclassification. Because the paper relies on these reconstructed values to argue temporality and strengthen the association, the main conclusion that low pre-infection vitamin D predicts worse COVID-19 outcomes is not supported. More broadly, the analysis cannot distinguish true vitamin D effect from confounding by health status, healthcare-seeking, indication for testing, ethnicity, obesity, frailty, and chronic disease; but the specific retraction-triggering flaw is the invalid methodological treatment of historical vitamin D data as patient-specific predicted exposure.
correct_approach: The authors should have avoided using a single cohort-level cosinor curve to estimate individual vitamin D levels at hospitalization from one prior measurement. Correct approaches would include: (1) analyze only actually observed 25(OH)D measurements obtained sufficiently close to infection using a prespecified short window; (2) if repeated measurements exist, use within-person longitudinal modeling to estimate individual trajectories; (3) otherwise treat time-from-measurement as a major source of uncertainty and adjust/stratify by it rather than imputing precise patient-specific values; (4) present any seasonal correction only as a group-level sensitivity analysis, not as reconstructed individual exposure; and (5) avoid causal language unless supported by a design that adequately addresses confounding and measurement validity.
additional_errors: Strong selection bias: only 253 of 1176 hospitalized patients had prior vitamin D testing, and those tested were likely systematically different from those not tested.
Confounding by indication and residual confounding: prior vitamin D testing and low vitamin D are associated with age, comorbidity, frailty, obesity, ethnicity, and health behavior.
Use of backward stepwise logistic regression can yield unstable estimates and inflated effect sizes.
Very wide confidence intervals around the main odds ratio suggest imprecision and potential sparse-data bias.
The paper overstates causality from a retrospective observational association.
Historical measurements up to 730 days before infection are too remote to define baseline exposure reliably, even without the cosinor issue.
Possible inappropriate dichotomization/categorization of continuous variables leading to information loss and unstable comparisons.
